steps.action: The kujawiak is a Polish folk dance from the reg with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat.
steps.text: The kujawiak is a Polish folk dance from the reg with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat.

steps.action: It is one of the five national dances of Poland,
steps.text: It is one of the five national dances of Poland, the others being the krakowiak, mazur, oberek, and polonaise.

steps.action: The dance typically involves couples walking gra
steps.text: The dance typically involves couples walking gracefully in a quarter-note rhythm, on slightly bended knees, with relaxed turns, around a circle.

steps.action: It is argued that the dance was developed from t
steps.text: It is argued that the dance was developed from the mazur between 1750 and 1830.

steps.action: The kujawiak involved much collaboration between
steps.text: The kujawiak involved much collaboration between dancers and musicians in olden times.

steps.action: The dancer would request a specific tune by sing
steps.text: The dancer would request a specific tune by singing it to the band, often filling lyrical lines with "dana, dana" as an imitation of the sound of an instrument.

steps.action: The first fiddler would then guess how to play t
steps.text: The first fiddler would then guess how to play the tune from the gestures and movements of the dancer, and the dancer would give the musicians some money.

steps.action: Though the dance began in the villages and peasa
steps.text: Though the dance began in the villages and peasantry, it was embraced by the landed gentry and nobility due to its beauty.

steps.action: They collected and published Kujawiak melodies,
steps.text: They collected and published Kujawiak melodies, and invited the village musicians and dancers to their manors to learn the dance.

steps.action: From there, the dance moved to festivities in bi
steps.text: From there, the dance moved to festivities in big cities like Warsaw, and spread all over Poland.

steps.action: As the dance grew in popularity, it also grew in
steps.text: As the dance grew in popularity, it also grew in embellishments, taking on alternating tempos, show-off steps, and new figures.

steps.action: It was adopted as a Polish national dance at the
steps.text: It was adopted as a Polish national dance at the end of the 19th century.

steps.action: In 1990, Czesław Sroka published Polskie tańce n
steps.text: In 1990, Czesław Sroka published Polskie tańce narodowe – systematyka (Polish National Dances: A Typology).

steps.action: The dancers would perform in pairs, whirling alo
steps.text: The dancers would perform in pairs, whirling along a circle, led by a leader or leading pair.

steps.action: It was commonly danced during weddings and villa
steps.text: It was commonly danced during weddings and village dance parties.

steps.action: The kujawiak exists in two forms: as a regional
steps.text: The kujawiak exists in two forms: as a regional folk dance inclusive of its many variants, and as a unified "national dance".

steps.action: For both, it is characterized by its rotations o
steps.text: For both, it is characterized by its rotations of couples, shifting around a large, circular dance space.

steps.action: The folk versions are faster and more complicate
steps.text: The folk versions are faster and more complicated than its national counterpart.

steps.action: Kujawiak was originally often performed as a sui
steps.text: Kujawiak was originally often performed as a suite of three dances called "okrągły", meaning round.

steps.action: The dances were each of a different tempo, yet a
steps.text: The dances were each of a different tempo, yet all featured the mazurka rhythm and a triple meter.

steps.action: The kujawiak was preceded by fifteen to thirty m
steps.text: The kujawiak was preceded by fifteen to thirty minutes of a slow walking dance, like the chodzony, with partners holding each other by their sides and outlining the dancing area.

steps.action: The suite ended with a quick turning dance, the
steps.text: The suite ended with a quick turning dance, the mazurek or the oberek.

steps.action: In some regions, the order of dances might be re
steps.text: In some regions, the order of dances might be reversed (mazur-kujawiak-oberek) with a decelerating tempo.

steps.action: The kujawiak's steps are performed on relaxed, s
steps.text: The kujawiak's steps are performed on relaxed, slightly bent knees, with toes placed on the floor first before the rest of the foot.
